ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a composite Thai snack platter. FOODON_03315091 (chicken wing food product) is used as the closest parent anchor for the dominant protein component. The crispy fish and meat ball components are not individually represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Poultry meat and poultry meat products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Poultry meat and poultry meat products) is the closest defensible parent for this mixed platter. No composite Thai snack platter code exists in FoodEx2. The fish and meat ball components fall under separate FoodEx2 branches not captured by a single code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept exists for a composite Thai snack platter of this type.

serving.description: 1 sharing platter (36 pieces: 12 chicken wings/drumettes, 12 crispy fish, 12 meat balls)
serving.natural_language_portion: One sharing platter contains approximately 720 g total, comprising 12 chicken wings and drumettes (approximately 300 g), 12 pieces of crispy fried fish (approximately 180 g), and 12 beef or chicken balls (approximately 240 g), intended for 3–4 people.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken wing, cooked, fried (NDB 05064); Fish, battered or breaded, fried (NDB 15209); Beef meatball, cooked (NDB 22904) — weighted composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for a composite Thai snack platter exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of three USDA FoodData Central entries: (1) Chicken wing, cooked, fried (USDA SR Legacy NDB 05064) weighted at ~42% of total mass; (2) Fish, battered and fried (USDA SR Legacy NDB 15209) weighted at ~25% of total mass; (3) Beef meatball, cooked (USDA SR Legacy NDB 22904) weighted at ~33% of total mass. Thai marinade and seasoning contributions to sodium and sugar are estimated from typical Thai marinade profiles. All per-100g values represent the blended composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on fried chicken and fish batter components
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Thai snack platter. GI is estimated at 45 (low category) based on analogous fried chicken and battered fish entries in Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN). Fried chicken with coating has a reported GI of approximately 40–50 due to the moderate starch content of the batter and the fat-slowing effect on gastric emptying. Meat balls contribute minimal glycemic load. The blended GI of 45 is a defensible conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 53.3) / 100 ≈ 24.0, where carbohydrate per 720 g serving = 7.4 × 7.2 = 53.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~3456 mg per full platter) from marinades, sauces, and seasoned coatings
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this platter due to its very high sodium load, which substantially exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg sodium; if consumed, portion should be restricted to a small serving of 2–3 pieces with no additional salty condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content from battered coatings combined with high fat content, which may contribute to postprandial glucose elevation and caloric excess
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 3–4 pieces (approximately 100–120 g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the glycemic and caloric impact of the fried batter coatings and high-fat meat components.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~4.1 g per 100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g per 100 g), cholesterol (~78 mg per 100 g), and very high sodium from deep-frying and seasoned marinades
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this platter as an occasional indulgence only, given its combination of saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and very high sodium, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; a single full platter provides approximately 30 g saturated fat and over 3400 mg sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Thai Snack Platter (Chicken Wings, Crispy Fish, Meat Balls) is a generously portioned Thai-inspired sharing platter served at Saap Saap Thai restaurant in Singapore. It comprises 12 chicken wings and drumettes, 12 pieces of crispy fried fish, and 12 beef or chicken balls, totalling approximately 720 g. The chicken wings and drumettes are marinated in Thai spices — typically lemongrass, galangal, fish sauce, and chilli — then deep-fried to achieve a golden, crispy exterior with juicy, flavourful meat inside. The crispy fish pieces are coated in a light seasoned batter and deep-fried until crunchy. The meat balls, made from minced beef or chicken, are seasoned with Thai aromatics and either steamed or fried. The overall flavour profile is savoury, umami-rich, mildly spicy, and aromatic, with contrasting textures of crispy exteriors and tender interiors. This platter is designed for group sharing as a starter or snack course. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, high-fat, high-sodium food with moderate carbohydrate content from the battered coatings. The estimated energy density is approximately 231 kcal per 100 g, with 17.8 g protein, 14.2 g fat, and 7.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The very high sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) makes it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low category), but the glycemic load per full platter is substantial (~24) due to the large serving size. Clinically, this platter is best consumed in small portions by individuals with diet-sensitive conditions.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for this composite Thai snack platter exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated from a weighted composite of three USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried chicken wing, battered fried fish, and cooked beef meatball. The GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (fried chicken wing NDB 05064, battered fried fish NDB 15209, cooked beef meatball NDB 22904) in approximate mass proportions of 42:25:33. No direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are estimated from the weighted composite of the three USDA source entries; individual values carry moderate uncertainty. 4. Sodium estimate includes a +80 mg per 100 g uplift above the raw USDA composite to account for Thai marinade and seasoning contributions typical of restaurant preparation, based on FAO/INFOODS SEA seasoning data. 5. The halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed from available public information for Saap Saap Thai - Funan; consumers requiring halal certification should verify directly with the restaurant. 6. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite platter exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 7. The full platter at 720 g provides approximately 1663 kcal, 128 g protein, 102 g fat, and 53 g carbohydrate — a very high-calorie shared meal. 8. trans_fat_g of 0.2 per 100 g is estimated from deep-frying oil contribution in the USDA fried chicken wing entry.
